findings.summary: infrastructure/core/multi_project.py imports from infrastructure/reporting inside a method body, blurring the core→reporting direction at runtime
evidence: Line 280: `from infrastructure.reporting import generate_multi_project_report` is a deferred import inside a method — this is a core module importing from a higher-level reporting module, violating the expected dependency direction (core should not know about reporting)
findings.suggestion: Invert the dependency: the reporting layer or the multi-project orchestration layer above core should own the call to generate_multi_project_report and pass results back; core should not reach into reporting

findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py imports from infrastructure/validation, coupling two sibling layers
evidence: Line 20: `from infrastructure.validation.integrity import verify_academic_standards` — publishing imports validation; these are sibling subsystems and the coupling creates a hidden dependency edge that is not documented in the architecture
findings.suggestion: Either accept both as co-equal infrastructure siblings and document the edge, or extract verify_academic_standards into infrastructure/core (if truly generic) so publishing can import it from the lower layer

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/markdown_validator.py uses os.path throughout while every sibling infrastructure module uses pathlib exclusively
evidence: Lines 109-143: os.path.dirname, os.path.join, os.path.normpath, os.path.isabs, os.path.exists, os.path.basename, os.path.relpath used across validate_images(); the rest of the infrastructure codebase (config_loader.py, environment.py, pipeline.py, publishing/core.py, etc.) operates exclusively on pathlib.Path objects
findings.suggestion: Replace the os.path calls with pathlib equivalents to match the project-wide convention; Path.parent, Path.joinpath, Path.exists(), Path.name, Path.relative_to() cover all usages here

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/validate_markdown_cli.py re-implements find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, and validate_math locally despite identical functions existing in markdown_validator.py
evidence: Lines 55-176 of validate_markdown_cli.py define find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, validate_math as standalone functions with comment 'for testing'; the identical function names exist in markdown_validator.py with different signatures (list[str] vs Set/Tuple) — this is a shadow implementation that diverges from the canonical module
findings.suggestion: Remove the shadow functions from the CLI file and import directly from markdown_validator for tests; the comment 'for testing' is not a valid reason to maintain duplicates

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/config_cli.py has an if/else branch where both arms execute the same code
evidence: Lines 87-91: `if key == 'AUTHOR_DETAILS': value_escaped = value.replace('"', '\\"') else: value_escaped = value.replace('"', '\\"')` — both branches are identical; the comment says 'Preserve backslashes for LaTeX in AUTHOR_DETAILS' but no difference is implemented
findings.suggestion: Remove the if/else and keep a single assignment; if the intent was different escaping for AUTHOR_DETAILS, implement it

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/dashboard_generator.py has `import csv` between two matplotlib imports, breaking the standard import ordering convention
evidence: Lines 14-17: `import matplotlib` then `import csv` then `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt` — csv is a stdlib module inserted between two lines of the same third-party import group; every other file groups stdlib and third-party imports conventionally
findings.suggestion: Move `import csv` to the stdlib import block above the matplotlib imports

findings.summary: infrastructure/llm/core/context.py logs entry and exit on every mutation method with full state dumps
evidence: add_message() emits two logger.debug calls with full object state (lines 47-57 and 67-77); _prune_context() emits logger.debug on every individual message removed in a while-loop (lines 144-154) and an additional logger.info on completion; clear() emits logger.info before and logger.debug after a two-line operation (lines 103-115); restore_state() emits logger.info and logger.debug bracketing three assignment lines (lines 233-251)
findings.suggestion: Collapse to a single log per public method at DEBUG level; remove the per-iteration prune debug log from the loop body — it generates O(n) log entries per prune operation

findings.summary: LLMClient.query() and query_raw() log entry/exit with full state at INFO level for every single LLM call
evidence: query(): logger.info 'Starting query' with model+prompt_length, then logger.info 'Query completed' with model+response_length+generation_time — routine INFO logging for every invocation; query_raw() computes prompt_preview (line 294) but then logs prompt_length rather than the preview, making the preview variable unused in the normal path (lines 296-305)
findings.suggestion: Lower routine per-call logging to DEBUG; the prompt_preview variable in query_raw is computed but not used in the logged fields — either log it or remove the computation

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/environment.py has 3-8 line docstrings on trivial delegation functions that restate the obvious
evidence: get_python_command() (lines 316-335): 19-line docstring for a 1-line function `return [sys.executable]`; check_uv_available() (lines 291-313): 11-line docstring with usage example for a 5-line subprocess call; set_environment_variables() (lines 456-494): docstring includes a usage example that just asserts `success is True` — adds no information
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings to one sentence stating what the function returns; remove trivial examples that just call the function

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/environment.py calls log_success() on every individual package check, tool check, and directory creation
evidence: check_dependencies(): log_success() called inside a for-loop for every package found (lines 100, 109); check_build_tools(): log_success() inside for-loop for each tool (line 221); setup_directories(): log_success() inside for-loop for each directory created (line 284) — in a typical setup this generates dozens of INFO-level lines for entirely expected outcomes
findings.suggestion: Log a single summary at INFO level after the loop (e.g., 'All N packages found'); use DEBUG inside the loop for per-item confirmation

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py claims to be a 'THIN ORCHESTRATOR' but contains substantial business logic duplicating its own infrastructure module
evidence: Module docstring line 2: 'This script validates markdown files… All business logic is in infrastructure/markdown_validator.py / This script handles only CLI argument parsing and I/O'; however lines 55-176 implement find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, validate_math with full regex logic — the file contradicts its own stated contract
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicated functions; if tests need direct access to those primitives they should import from markdown_validator, not from the CLI file

findings.summary: scripts/__init__.py MENU_SCRIPT_MAPPING references script entry point names (function field) that don't match the actual functions in the target scripts
evidence: Entry 0 lists function='run_setup_environment', entry 2 lists function='run_analysis_standalone', entry 4 lists function='run_validation_standalone' — these are symbolic names with no verified binding to actual callables; the mapping is documentation that cannot be validated programmatically and can drift silently from the actual script entry points
findings.suggestion: Either remove the function field (it provides false precision if not verified) or replace it with the actual callable reference imported from each script so drift is caught at import time

findings.summary: load_config swallows all unexpected exceptions with a bare warning, losing error context entirely
evidence: Lines 159-162: bare `except Exception:` logs a single warning line and returns None, discarding the original exception type and traceback. Callers cannot distinguish a missing file from a YAML parse error from an encoding fault.
findings.suggestion: At minimum chain the original exception into context: `logger.warning('Unexpected error loading config from %s: %s', config_path, e)` is already there but the exception should be re-raised or wrapped in a ConfigurationError so callers can branch on type.

findings.summary: I/O boundary functions inconsistently return None/False vs raise on failure; callers must guess which contract applies
evidence: config_loader.load_config returns None on file-not-found, YAML error, and unexpected error — three distinct failure modes collapsed to one sentinel.
environment.setup_directories returns False on exception (line 286-288), while validate_uv_sync_result returns a (bool, str) tuple (lines 519-527).
integrity.load_integrity_manifest returns None on any failure (lines 710-714) with no error type communicated.
environment.check_build_tools returns False on tool-not-found — no exception, no message to caller beyond the logged text.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single boundary contract: either raise typed exceptions (ConfigurationError, FileOperationError) for unrecoverable errors and reserve None/False for 'legitimately absent', or use a consistent Result/Optional pattern with logged rationale. Do not mix.

findings.summary: verify_cross_references and verify_academic_standards use bare `except Exception` that swallows read errors and continues or returns a poisoned dict
evidence: Lines 131-134: `except Exception as e: logger.error(...); continue` — file read failures are logged and skipped; the partial label/reference sets are used for cross-reference checks, producing false-pass results.
Lines 237-243: a single file read failure causes verify_academic_standards to return all-False without examining the remaining files.
findings.suggestion: For verify_cross_references, propagate the failure in the integrity dict rather than silently continuing. For verify_academic_standards, accumulate content from successfully-read files and only mark keys False for sections that could not be checked, not all keys.

findings.summary: PipelineExecutor._execute_stage catches all exceptions and converts them to a failed PipelineStageResult, losing exception type at the boundary
evidence: Lines 286-296: `except Exception as e:` converts any exception to `error_message=str(e)` in PipelineStageResult. The original exception class is discarded; callers who want to re-raise or branch by type cannot do so.
findings.suggestion: Store `exception_type=type(e).__name__` in PipelineStageResult, or re-raise after logging for truly unexpected exceptions. The current pattern hides bugs (e.g. a TypeError in a stage function looks the same as a deliberate ScriptExecutionError).

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py re-implements image, ref, and math validation logic that already exists in markdown_validator.py
evidence: validate_markdown_cli.py lines 55-176 define find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, validate_math — each with a comment 'for testing'.
markdown_validator.py already exports identically-named functions with the same signatures and logic.
The CLI versions differ in small ways (collect_symbols returns dict/dict instead of Set/Set) — divergence that will silently produce different results.
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicated functions from validate_markdown_cli.py. Tests should import directly from markdown_validator. The 'for testing' wrappers introduce silent signature drift.

findings.summary: rendering/__init__.py get_render_manager is a zero-policy pass-through wrapper with no added behaviour
evidence: Lines 22-31: `def get_render_manager(config=None): return RenderManager(config)` — the function does nothing except instantiate RenderManager. It adds no retry, no default config injection beyond what RenderManager.__init__ already handles, no logging.
findings.suggestion: Delete get_render_manager and have callers instantiate RenderManager directly. If default config injection is needed, put it in RenderManager.__init__.

findings.summary: _load_project_config is a thin wrapper around find_config_file + load_config that adds no policy
evidence: Lines 291-298: _load_project_config constructs a path, checks exists, and calls load_config — the exact same three lines are duplicated in get_testing_config (lines 448-450) which does not use the helper.
The helper saves two lines but is called only twice and inconsistently.
findings.suggestion: Either use _load_project_config consistently everywhere (including get_testing_config) or inline it. The inconsistency is worse than either choice.

findings.summary: reporting/__init__.py re-exports 28 symbols from 7 sibling submodules with an alias collision
evidence: Lines 15-49: imports generate_test_report from pipeline_reporter and generate_test_report_from_results (aliased from coverage_reporter.generate_test_report) — both are re-exported under different names from the same __init__.
The __all__ list (lines 113-140) includes both names, making it non-obvious which to use for a given purpose.
28 public symbols surfaced at package level obscures which submodule owns each capability.
findings.suggestion: Stop re-exporting coverage_reporter functions through the package init; callers that need parse_pytest_output should import from infrastructure.reporting.coverage_reporter directly. Reserve __init__.py for the multi-project orchestration function that genuinely needs a stable path.

findings.summary: integrity.py mixes file hashing, cross-reference validation, academic-standard checking, permission testing, manifest I/O, and report generation in one module
evidence: Functions in a single file: verify_file_integrity, verify_cross_references, verify_data_consistency, verify_academic_standards, verify_output_integrity, generate_integrity_report, validate_build_artifacts, check_file_permissions, verify_output_completeness, create_integrity_manifest, save_integrity_manifest, load_integrity_manifest, verify_integrity_against_manifest — 13 public functions.
check_file_permissions (lines 482-519) writes a .permission_test sentinel file as a side effect inside a validation module.
verify_academic_standards (lines 222-265) duplicates regex-based section detection that also exists in publishing/core.py validate_publication_readiness.
findings.suggestion: Split into at least: file_integrity.py (hashing + manifest I/O), cross_reference_checker.py (label/ref validation), academic_standards.py (section presence checks). check_file_permissions is an environment concern and belongs in environment.py or a dedicated permissions helper.

findings.summary: PipelineExecutor.__init__ and _setup_log_file_handler mix pipeline orchestration with logging infrastructure management, violating SRP
evidence: PipelineExecutor.__init__ (lines 74-96) sets up a file log handler, stores it as self._log_handler, and calls _setup_log_file_handler.

_setup_log_file_handler (lines 98-143) inspects the root logger's handler list, removes stale handlers, creates a new FileHandler, and configures its formatter — all concerns that belong in a LoggingManager or setup_logger utility.
_flush_log_handlers (lines 529-563) iterates all logger instances in logging.Manager.loggerDict — a global state traversal inside a pipeline class.
_run_copy_outputs (lines 519-527) calls _flush_log_handlers before delegating to a script, coupling copy semantics to logging semantics.
findings.suggestion: Extract log file lifecycle management into a standalone LogFileHandler context manager or extend infrastructure/core/logging_utils.py with a setup_pipeline_log_file helper. PipelineExecutor should hold only an opaque handle and call flush/close on it.

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py claims to be a thin orchestrator but implements four full validation functions not used by main()
evidence: The module docstring (line 3) states 'All business logic is in infrastructure/markdown_validator.py / This script handles only CLI argument parsing and I/O.'
Lines 55-176 implement find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, validate_math — 120 lines of business logic contradicting the stated contract.
main() (lines 179-229) does not call any of these four functions; they exist only for test consumers.
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicated logic. Expose the needed functions from markdown_validator.py for tests and import them there.

findings.summary: dashboard_generator.py imports matplotlib at module level without guarding, causing import-time side effects in contexts where matplotlib is unavailable
evidence: Lines 14-19: `import matplotlib`, `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt`, `import numpy as np`, `from matplotlib.figure import Figure` are unconditional top-level imports.
Line 14: `import matplotlib` appears before `import csv` — the ordering suggests these were added ad-hoc.

The rest of the infrastructure uses try/except ImportError guards for optional heavy deps (e.g. integrity.py lines 23-27 guard numpy, config_loader.py lines 93-98 guard yaml). dashboard_generator breaks this convention.
findings.suggestion: Wrap matplotlib and numpy imports in try/except ImportError with MATPLOTLIB_AVAILABLE / NUMPY_AVAILABLE flags, or use lazy imports inside each chart function. This is consistent with how the rest of the infrastructure handles optional scientific deps.

findings.summary: _resume_pipeline has three structurally identical fallback blocks for starting fresh, creating complex control flow
evidence: Lines 306-317: invalid checkpoint fallback with self._resuming guard + try/finally.
Lines 319-330: missing checkpoint fallback — identical pattern.
Lines 369-381: stage mismatch fallback — identical pattern.

Each block sets self._resuming=True, calls execute_full_pipeline or execute_core_pipeline based on skip_llm, then resets in finally. The ternary `execute_full_pipeline() if not self.config.skip_llm else execute_core_pipeline()` is repeated three times.
findings.suggestion: Extract a `_fresh_run() -> list[PipelineStageResult]` helper that handles the self._resuming guard and the skip_llm branch, then call it from each fallback site. This eliminates the three copies and makes the guard logic testable in isolation.

findings.summary: publishing/core.py imports from validation/integrity.py at module load, creating a cross-layer coupling that is not obvious from the module docstring
evidence: Line 21: `from infrastructure.validation.integrity import verify_academic_standards` — publishing imports from validation at module level.

validate_publication_readiness (lines 250-293) calls verify_academic_standards to compute a completeness score, but the same academic-standard regex detection is also embedded inline in integrity.py. The result is two implementations of section-presence detection.
findings.suggestion: The academic standards check used by both modules should live in a single, shared location (e.g. a new validation/academic_standards.py). publishing/core.py and integrity.py should both import from there rather than duplicating or creating a validation->publishing->validation cycle risk.

findings.summary: requests is a core runtime dependency but is used exclusively for Ollama HTTP calls in the LLM layer
evidence: pyproject.toml line 44: 'requests>=2.31.0' listed as a top-level runtime dependency
infrastructure/llm/core/client.py line 21: 'import requests' — the only non-test consumer of the requests library at module level

The ollama dependency group (pyproject.toml line 61) is optional, but requests which supports it is listed as required — requests is heavy (brings urllib3, certifi, charset-normalizer, idna) for what is essentially one HTTP endpoint call
findings.suggestion: Move 'requests' into the optional 'llm' dependency group alongside 'ollama'. The LLM client already gracefully degrades (PROMPT_LOADER_AVAILABLE flag), so the import can be made conditional or requests replaced with stdlib urllib for simple POST calls.

findings.summary: pillow is a core runtime dependency but its use appears limited to optional steganography and image overlay features
evidence: pyproject.toml line 45: 'pillow>=10.0.0' listed as a required runtime dependency
pyproject.toml line 72: 'qrcode[pil]>=8.2' already depends on pillow for the optional steganography group — pillow is pulled in regardless
pillow is a large binary wheel (~4MB) pulled in unconditionally even for users who never use figure overlays or steganography
findings.suggestion: Evaluate whether pillow is genuinely needed on all install paths. If its only required use is in optional overlay/steganography code, move it to the 'steganography' optional group and guard the import sites with try/except.

findings.summary: scipy is listed as an 'optional' package in environment.py default checks but is not declared in pyproject.toml at all
evidence: infrastructure/core/environment.py lines 90-91: optional_packages = ['scipy'] — checked and warned about during pipeline setup
pyproject.toml has no scipy entry in dependencies or any dependency group
This creates a latent dependency that users are warned about but have no declarative way to install via uv
findings.suggestion: Either add scipy as an optional dependency group in pyproject.toml (e.g. '[dependency-groups] scientific = ["scipy"]') or remove it from the environment check if it is no longer needed.

findings.summary: Several high-value functions in environment.py have no test coverage
evidence: test_environment.py only imports and tests check_python_version and check_dependencies

Functions with no test coverage in environment.py: install_missing_packages (lines 121-197), setup_directories (lines 261-288), get_subprocess_env (lines 381-402), validate_interpreter (lines 338-378), validate_uv_sync_result (lines 497-527), validate_directory_structure (lines 530-558)
setup_directories and validate_directory_structure are called by the pipeline on every run — these are critical paths
test_environment.py TestEnvironmentSetupIntegration tests mkdir via tmp_path directly, not via the actual setup_directories() function
findings.suggestion: Add tests for setup_directories(tmp_path, 'test_project'), validate_directory_structure(tmp_path, 'test_project'), and get_subprocess_env() using real temp directories. validate_uv_sync_result can be tested by creating .venv and uv.lock files in tmp_path.

findings.summary: projects/code_project/tests/ contains only one test file covering one src module
evidence: Glob of projects/code_project/tests/ returns only: __init__.py, conftest.py, test_optimizer.py
test_optimizer.py imports from src.optimizer only — no other src modules are tested
If code_project/src/ has more than one module, those modules have zero coverage from the project test suite
findings.suggestion: Audit projects/code_project/src/ for additional modules and add corresponding test files to reach the stated 90% project coverage requirement.

findings.summary: test_health_check.py uses time.sleep and asserts timestamp ordering, creating a fragile timing-dependent test
evidence: test_health_check.py line 117: time.sleep(0.01)
Line 118-119: assert status2['timestamp'] >= status1['timestamp'] — the assertion uses >= not > meaning the sleep is required to distinguish timestamps but the assertion is too weak to require it
findings.suggestion: The test intent (fresh timestamps per call) is valid but relies on wall-clock time. Assert that timestamp is a float close to time.time() instead, or remove the sleep and only assert the type is float > 0.

findings.summary: Infrastructure-dependent test classes in test_optimizer.py use pytest.skip() when infrastructure is unavailable, silently dropping coverage
evidence: Lines 20-35: INFRASTRUCTURE_AVAILABLE flag set via try/except import from scripts/optimization_analysis.py
Lines 440-442, 489-491, 538-540: @pytest.mark.skipif(not INFRASTRUCTURE_AVAILABLE, ...) decorates three test classes
Lines 451-466, 469-484, 495-515, etc.: individual test bodies also call pytest.skip() if result_path is None — double-guarded skips
This means TestStabilityAnalysis, TestPerformanceBenchmarking, TestAnalysisDashboard can all silently skip in CI without any failure signal
findings.suggestion: Replace soft INFRASTRUCTURE_AVAILABLE skip with a hard fixture that asserts the scripts are importable. If the scripts are part of the project, they should always be importable in the test environment and failures should be test failures, not skips.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/ contains 22 Python files with mixed concerns — document scanning, link checking, PDF validation, and audit orchestration are all co-located
evidence: Glob of infrastructure/validation/*.py returns 22 files: known_exceptions.py, mock_validator.py, figure_validator.py, link_validator.py, output_validator.py, doc_scanner.py, doc_models.py, audit_orchestrator.py, issue_categorizer.py, doc_accuracy.py, doc_discovery.py, repo_scanner.py, doc_completeness.py, doc_quality.py, markdown_validator.py, check_links.py, __init__.py, cli.py, pdf_validator.py, integrity.py, validate_pdf_cli.py, validate_markdown_cli.py

The directory mixes at least 4 distinct sub-concerns: (1) PDF/markdown validation, (2) document scanning/accuracy (doc_scanner, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, doc_quality, doc_models, doc_discovery), (3) link checking (link_validator, check_links, check_links.py), (4) audit orchestration (audit_orchestrator, issue_categorizer, known_exceptions, repo_scanner)
infrastructure/validation/__init__.py re-exports 28 symbols, making the public surface opaque
findings.suggestion: Split into sub-packages: validation/document/ (doc_scanner, doc_models, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, doc_quality, doc_discovery), validation/links/ (link_validator, check_links), validation/audit/ (audit_orchestrator, issue_categorizer, known_exceptions, repo_scanner). This reduces the flat file count from 22 to ~5 per sub-package.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/mock_validator.py has a name that implies it provides mock validation objects, but it actually enforces a no-mocks policy
evidence: mock_validator.py line 1-5: module docstring says 'Validates that no mock objects or frameworks are used in tests, enforcing the No Mocks Policy'
The file name 'mock_validator' reads as 'a validator that is a mock' or 'provides mock validators', not 'validates that mocks are absent'
The module is not exported from infrastructure/validation/__init__.py — it is a navigation dead-end for anyone browsing the package surface
findings.suggestion: Rename to no_mock_enforcer.py or mock_policy_checker.py to make the intent explicit. Also either add it to __init__.py exports or move it to a test utilities location since it scans test directories.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains 27 Python files with logging split across 5 separate modules
evidence: Glob of infrastructure/core/*.py returns 27 files
Logging concern is fragmented across: logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_progress.py — 5 files for one cross-cutting concern
Additional peripheral concerns: menu.py, cli.py, config_cli.py are CLI entry-points co-located with core utilities like retry.py, security.py, health_check.py
findings.suggestion: Consolidate logging sub-modules under infrastructure/core/logging/ sub-package. Move CLI-adjacent files (menu.py, cli.py, config_cli.py) to infrastructure/cli/ to reduce core's scope.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/mock_validator.py imports get_logger inside an exception handler rather than at module level
evidence: mock_validator.py lines 69-72: inside the except block of validate_no_mocks(), 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' and 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' are called on every exception
This means a new logger is instantiated on every file-read failure, and the import is re-executed each time the except branch runs
Every other infrastructure module imports get_logger at module level (e.g. config_loader.py line 15, environment.py line 45)
findings.suggestion: Move 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' and 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' to module level, consistent with all other infrastructure modules.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py imports get_logger inside the generate_multi_project_report function body
evidence: reporting/__init__.py line 80: 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' is inside the generate_multi_project_report() function body
Line 82: 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' — logger is created inside the function on every call
The module already has top-level imports from 7+ submodules; adding a module-level logger would be consistent
findings.suggestion: Move the get_logger import and logger instantiation to module level in reporting/__init__.py, matching the pattern used by all infrastructure submodules.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/config_loader.py has a bare except Exception that discards the exception type after already catching specific exceptions
evidence: config_loader.py lines 157-162: load_config() catches '(FileNotFoundError, PermissionError, yaml.YAMLError)' specifically, then falls through to 'except Exception: logger.warning(...); return None'

The broad except on line 159 swallows any unexpected exception (e.g. MemoryError, KeyboardInterrupt caught transitively) with only a warning log — the caller gets None with no signal about what went wrong
The warning message at line 161 does not include the exception itself: 'logger.warning(f"Unexpected error loading config from {config_path}")' — exception is silently dropped
findings.suggestion: Change the broad except to log the exception: 'logger.warning(f"Unexpected error loading config from {config_path}: {e}", exc_info=True)'. Consider narrowing to 'except (OSError, ValueError)' if those are the only realistic unexpected cases.

findings.summary: config_loader.get_testing_config() mixes walrus-operator style for env vars with explicit if/else for config file values in consecutive code blocks
evidence: Lines 432-442: env var extraction uses 'if env_infra := os.environ.get(...)' walrus pattern
Lines 452-461: config file extraction uses '(v := _safe_int_from_dict(...)) is not None' walrus pattern inside if-statements with explicit flag checks

Line 458-459: 'if not env_set_infra and (v := _safe_int_from_dict(testing_cfg, "infra_coverage_threshold")) is not None:' — the compound boolean with walrus and a boolean guard on the same line is difficult to parse at a glance
The two env-var boolean flags (env_set_infra, env_set_project) on lines 444-445 are set after the parsing loop rather than before, forcing the reader to mentally separate read from use
findings.suggestion: Extract env-var priority logic into a small helper that returns the resolved threshold value given env-var name, config dict key, and default. This would flatten the compound conditionals and make the priority chain readable in sequence.
